speaker: interviewer
text: Faigie, we were talking about the German occupation of Lithuania in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: In June of 1941. Yeah. And as I said, it was a real-- to me personally, it was a devastating shock, because all of a sudden, everybody was against us. We were-- because the Jews had a very good life, maybe the Lithuanians were jealous. As I said, the Jews had a very good life, and all of a sudden, we were hunted. My father-- well, the first thing, they were rounding up men, and they-- especially the intellectuals. I remember him calling my mother on the phone that they took away my father. They brought him back. They sent him back. But the other people weren't as lucky. If you-- random killing, random fires, and random looting. The Lithuanians, as I said before, were great helpers to the Germans. And it was a terrible shock to me having this safe cocoon now taken away. Not knowing, seeing soldiers marching, seeing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Nazis marching. I didn't-- I couldn't fathom it. I just couldn't. I couldn't-- I didn't understand what was happening. I remember asking my father, what's going on, because-- and he-- then he told me that-- that the people in Poland were-- were telling him what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- he told me that there were-- a lot of people were hurt. He-- I don't remember him telling me killed. I suppose he didn't want to scare me. But he was telling me that they were thrown out of their homes. I suppose he was trying to soften the blow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he explain this was an anti-Jewish thing that was happening in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I remember him telling me they don't like us. And I couldn't understand. We were so nice. How come they didn't like us? My mother was working in the hospital. She was such a terrific nurse. She was helping everybody. How come they didn't like us? And there was no solution. There was no answer to my question. It's just every day, something else bad happened. This one was killed. This one was killed. And I-- I suppose my nature, because I remember always saying, don't worry. It's going to be good. But it did affect me. But I still had my grandparents, and I still had my mother and father. And I still had some of my friends. And my friend, Chaya, I still had. And we used to sneak out and go visit each other. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still was alive, although destruction was going on all around me. Because I remember my mother telling me also that they bombed the Jewish hospital. And she was lucky. Mostly everybody was killed. She was lucky she was coming home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father was taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and brought back. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did he go away?

speaker: interviewee
text: --uh, a day or two days he was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought that this is-- that he is gone. That I won't see him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that feel like to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, terrible. Although my mother was still around, but I felt terrible. Because as I said, I was very, very close to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the German occupation of Kovno affect your mother and the other employees at the Jewish General?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's where I mean. The hospital was bombed. So then it started. I remember them coming and telling us about the ghetto. That the proclo--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they spoke in what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The Germans told the Lithuanians, and they must have told my father. Because one day, don't forget, they had Jewish people who were elected to be the head of the-- of the-- of the Jews. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: --very learned doctors and very known people that took over to help us, to help the administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the-- of Kovno. And they must have been told what to do, that the Jews have to move into a ghetto. They picked Slabodka, which was a suburb. And I remember my parents telling me that I will have to pick very few things because we have to take my stroller or wheelbarrows. We were not allowed. That the place was designated for us to move. We had to leave our home. I remember I couldn't understand what was-- what was happening. They gave us a certain time, I think in August, because it was still warm. And I remember it was after my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. I remember that was the saddest birthday I ever had. Remember from June, then comes July, and then comes August. Because I remember, in July, when I turned seven, in 1941, just my grandparents, we all got together very quietly to celebrate this joyous occasion that I always-- everybody made such a to-do with streamers, and with balloons, and the-- everybody from the little town was invited to come to celebrate at [INAUDIBLE] [GERMAN]. A very big deal was now a very solemn, quiet affair, just us and my grandparents and my aunt, Tanta Chava. And I don't even remember friends. Because I don't think people were venturing out, even-- only if they had to. And naturally, the yellow star started. You all wore a yellow star, and you didn't go out as much. Only if you had to. And then I knew already that we'll have to leave our home. So this was a very-- a terrible time to have-- to turn seven, when there was-- there was no joy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you move into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think around August, after my birthday. I remember taking my Shirley Temple doll. I remember getting dressed of layers and layers of clothing, because we were not allowed to-- there was no-- there were trucks and everything, but you weren't allowed to. You had to take-- or a wheelbarrow or a makeshift wagon. Or a-- like, I remember my carriage or my stroller, putting in all your clothes. So naturally, we dressed layer on top of layer. And we were told the Gentile population moved out of Slabodka, and the Jewish administration with, naturally, the Germans. Because everything was life and death. If they wouldn't have done it, they would have been shot. They tried very hard to cushion the blow. We moved in, about five or six people in a room, families in a little, little, in a little house, because Slabodka was not as cosmopolitan as Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from your area to Slabodka?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that feeling, walking through the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sad, very scared. Very, very scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the local population, were they around?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were around, smiling! Because don't forget, the-- the Jews were very well off. They were very learned. They were very well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Lithuanians took over everything. You couldn't take any furniture. You couldn't take anything. How would you carry it? You took your most prized possessions and your clothes. What had happened to your father's bookstore when the Germans took over?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took over. There was, like, he couldn't even go in there. Everything was confiscated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stopped working?

speaker: interviewee
text: He stopped working, oh. Everything was confiscated. For Jews, everything was confiscated if it was known that it was a Jewish place. So there was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything like Kristallnacht in Kovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember any breaking of-- of lights. I only remember that people were standing around and watching. Some of them were jeering-- could be-- and moving in this horrendous little house with other families. Very small. You have to understand. Maybe by other standards, it wasn't so small. But for me, it was very small, with other families moving in together. And right away, my father and all the men were forced to set up barbed wire, set up all around. That was the ghetto. My mother, luckily again, they opened a hospital. They made a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within the ghetto, right away. They had to-- to-- to-- there were sick people all over. And you weren't-- you wore a-- a yellow star. You weren't allowed out. Only on work detail. And it was terribly, terribly crowded. Terribly crowded. And I suppose now comes the story of the big action in October of 1941. They had to make place because we were terribly overcrowded. People were getting sick because it was very crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had an idea to get everybody out. They said that it was census-taking. There was a-- a larger ghetto and a small ghetto, and they said that some people will be sent to the smaller ghetto to make more room and more food, because we were starving.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my mother, again, my mother, covering her yellow star and going out with some of my clothes and bringing back food on the death-- under death of-- I mean, she would have been caught, she would have  been shot, nurse or no nurse. I remember my father couldn't do it. He was a softy like me. He couldn't do it. My mother did it a lot of times, not only this time, like, in the beginning. Because I had a lot of good clothes. And she went, covered her Star of David, and went in to-- to exchange for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know if she left the ghetto on that, or she traded within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no! Left the ghetto. With whom should she trade in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would have been really dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very dangerous. Very, very dangerous. I suppose she did it during the night. It was very dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto was completely fenced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely fenced in. Only working detail left through the gates. And there were-- there were guards all over, Lithuanians and Nazis, all over. And the people, naturally, they went out to work. Every morning, the men went to work, building the aerodrome, building highways. And they used to come back at night. And-- but I want to go back to October 1941. This will stay with me, I think, as long as I live. Everybody was told to go out for census-taking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uhnh! The-- the-- the soldiers, the Jewish administration. The rules came under penalty of death. Young, old, sick, no matter what, you were to-- even if you were very sick, you were taken out with a bed-- on a very, very big place. It was-- I don't remember the name, I can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: --no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken out--

speaker: interviewer
text: --through the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: --through the ghetto, on a very big-- it looked like an airfield, very huge. And everybody should line up in columns with their family. So all the families--

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were soldiers with guns--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with guns--

speaker: interviewer
text: --dogs--

speaker: interviewee
text: --soldiers, dogs, watching. And there was a little mountain and this terrible man that we used to see. We found out afterwards who he was. He was the Sergeant Major named Rauck, Helmut Rauck.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-L-M-U-T. Rauck, R-A-U-C-K. He was in the administration. And there were a lot of people in the German administration, but he was the most visible. We remembered him even walking through the ghetto. If he didn't like, you got always with a whip, you got hit over the head, or you could get killed. He was-- we were dreading him. If people knew that he was coming, everybody hid. And him-- and the first thing that we saw, him standing on the hill with gloves and a whip and a dog. And everybody had to line up, each family, naturally, together. And he-- it was called selections. They were telling that they were sending-- people shouldn't panic, because you had to go right and left because they were sending certain people to make more room for us, to give us more food, to-- in the smaller part of the ghetto-- to make a little bit more room that we should enjoy more.

speaker: interviewer
text: He spoke?

speaker: interviewee
text: He spoke, and that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in German. And he-- naturally, the Lithuanians helped him. And also the Jewish administration, because they didn't know anything what was happening. It was supposed to be a census-taking to the benefit of us. It turned out that recht and links-- right and left-- right was bad, left was good. And it-- in the beginning, you didn't know what was happening. But then you realized his major objective was to separate families. My mother and father and I were walking together-- and naturally, my father was a very tall, good-looking man-- nicely dressed. Because you have to remember, this was the beginning of the ghetto. It wasn't-- we still were nicely dressed, my mother very well, and me, naturally. My grandparents in front of us. I remember my grandfather, my aunt, and my grandmother, very austere, very-- she always held herself very high, so she looked very good. What he did, he sent my grandmother-- grandfather and my aunt to the left-- to the right, sorry. And we were all sent to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father, your mother, and yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and myself and my grandmother. Now, you didn't-- we were, I suppose, from the beginning-- so you didn't realize what was happening. You were walking. But I remember, before it all started, my stomach, going to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother was panicking because she didn't trust them. She saw what was happening in the hospital. She couldn't get over that they bombed her hospital, you see? So she was, and I-- that was the first time when I said, don't worry. It's going to be good. And I walked-- I mean, bombing a hospital is just against--

speaker: interviewee
text: Against her grain. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: --her code.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her code. So she said she was very worried. And I said, don't worry. [GERMAN]. And we were walking, and we were sent to the left. The only thing I couldn't, why my grandmother? So I figured, maybe we-- I suppose everybody figured, maybe there-- there will be help. My grandfather was a little bit older. And then we realized what was happening. Because slowly, when it was getting dark-- Kovno was surrounding by hills-- we saw columns and columns of people going up to the fort, going up and going away. And then we saw, because we were marched back into the ghetto, so everybody was running against the wires, against the barbed wire, yelling to their relatives, yelling because we knew then what was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the kind of things that people were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me say, first of all, people were yelling, [YIDDISH] and save yourself. You know what I mean? And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Yiddish. That was the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] horror of my life. Because my grandfather and my-- and we realized that they were-- that they were going to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You realized this, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. But everybody else did. And then when we were back in the ghetto, one person-- I think a little boy-- hid because-- and he told. And you see, via the grapevine, we found out that they were just massacred, and people were falling into ravines. And maybe that were some of the ravines that the-- the people-- the Jewish people dug for them. Because when they went on working details, you didn't know what you were going to do or what you were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he did. Helmut Rauck did thin out the ghetto. Over 10,000 people were lost. And he was smiling and going back and forth. And I think he also liked-- we-- later on you realized-- he liked cleanliness. So those who were-- who appeared very nice and young and strong were [NON-ENGLISH]. What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] means you could work. The young, young girls, and the old-- that was the first time-- and the sick were taken away. Then you realized. In the beginning, you didn't realize, because people didn't panic. People walked nicely and quietly at the end. But it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that people were throwing themselves against the wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Against the wire, yelling for their relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think everybody lost somebody that day. The idea was to demoralize us. Can you imagine taking away my young aunt? She was a young girl. She was maybe 17, 18. And leaving my grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Taking, you know? It's-- that was his job. The moment he-- when he walked-- I remember him-- I don't remember any other ones. I remember him. Stocky, very mean-looking, always with a whip. And everybody was deathly afraid of him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was performing all the duties. He was a staff sergeant, an SS, naturally, SS man. There were-- there were other Germans who had higher titles, but he was the whole chief cook and bottle washer, as we say. He was maybe-- he must have really enjoyed what he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: These people who were throwing themselves against the wires, how were they treated? What did the SS do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I suppose the other Jewish people took them away. Because again, survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the large ghetto and the small ghetto remained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remained. So the small ghetto, there was no small ghetto. There was the large ghetto and then, then again, then we lived again. And we tried-- my mother went to work at the hospital. In fact, there's [NON-ENGLISH]. He was the only Lithuanian Jew that I met in Toronto. I was thrilled, because from Kovno, there was count on your fingers. I met him at one of the gatherings at the Lipa Green Center, the Holocaust thing. I was so excited. And his mother, she passed away recently, remembered my mother. They worked together in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life started to go on. You know how it is. It goes on. The hospital was formed. We weren't allowed to study, so we used to hide. Somebody used to look, and we used to quietly study, quietly tell stories, quietly play. The men went to work. Your father went out every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out to work on the working detail. I don't know what he dug. I mean, the aerodrome, like I said before. They went to working detail. My mother stood inside, and me, with my friends. And I was with my friend Chaya-- her I'll always remember-- and other friends. We used to sing. It came through-- I don't remember when the Partisan song-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People were dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that song well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to sing that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's too emotional. Maybe later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember people dying in the streets. Hunger. Again, my mother covered her thing, and we had a little bit more because of the clothes that I had. And because she was at the hospital, so it was easier for her, maybe there was a little bit extra. I remember being very sick. That is also vivid.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Do you recall what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unh! In-- I don't remember the exact year. But during the ghetto time, being very, very sick with scarlet fever. And my mother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] dumping me from one bed to the other. She knew when the German doctors were coming through. Because otherwise, I would have been killed right away. It was a communicable disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were selections within the hospital, as well, as far as you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course!

speaker: interviewer
text: Ongoing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ongoing. Ongoing on any provocation. If you walked on the sidewalk, and you were supposed to walk in the ditch. If you walked on the ditch. Whatever there was, there were soldiers, there was especially, and Rauck, I remember him, that they picked on whatever. Now then comes-- now, another thing while we were in the ghetto. They needed-- you see, I'm talking--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we do that, just let's go back to that scarlet fever and what happened. How long were you in hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital quite a while--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you evaded the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I evaded because of my mother. Because remember, scarlet fever, you have things on your neck. So I remember being wrapped around-- around the neck and transferred from bed to bed. And don't forget, all the nurses looked after me. And then being all right. And again, they needed they-- a labor camp. They needed a labor camp further out than the ghetto. And we talk about miracles. Again, a miracle. That's-- I-- my life is full of miracles. I'll explain you the miracle what happened. My mother and my grandmother and my father were shipped out to a labor camp not far from Kovno, because they needed people to build again further down. [CLEARS THROAT] Excuse me. I remember that very vividly. And my mother also going to work. I suppose they needed a nurse while the people were working. One night will-- one day will always remain in my memory, again. The Jewish people, no matter what, were always singing, try to keep up the morale. I remember there was a concert, which very seldom you were allowed to, but I suppose they decided that they wanted us to have something. There was a concert. I stood very late at night, and I very seldom went out with my parents, out of the labor camp, to work. I was with my grandmother. I had my grandmother. I remember singing. I remember dancing. And I was very tired. The next day, my mother said to me, you're coming along with us. Come along with us. And I went along because she was with the infirm-- with the-- the nurse, so I was-- I went along with her. When we got back that night to the labor camp, there wasn't one child left. They came with trucks. And my grandmother, I lost my grandmother to them. So another miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Must've been '43, '42. Yeah, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes. Not-- not winter. It was springtime. And that's when they liquidated the children. And that was the-- the beginning, I suppose, a seed in my mother's head. They'll make me look older because [NON-ENGLISH] is very important. The children are taken away. I lost my grandmother, and I lost my friend Chaya. And I remember doing something when I got back. Taking my Shirley Temple doll and knocking it against the wall. This prized possession. I was so upset that I lost my grandmother. Maybe not just my grandmother, but I remember my friend Chaya. Chaya, and her mother was my mother's best friend. They took away all the children. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was explained to you? And who explained to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody explained. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was just gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was just gone. And I was so angry that-- you have to understand, I carried that Shirley Temple doll with me wherever I went. Because this was my-- my possession, my beautiful-- nobody had one. I don't remember anybody having. And I remember taking the doll and break into smithereens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember them saying anything to me. And then, for a certain time, we were still in labor camp. And then we were brought back into the ghetto. And we were in the ghetto till July of 1944. And again, people were dying. Maybe my nature that I had, my father wasn't depressed then because he had me and my mother. My mother worked at the hospital. How terrible things were, I had it better than a lot of people.
